annotations.label: Exponential distribution

anchor.snippet: the exponential distribution or negative exponential distribution is the probability distribution of the distance between events in a Poisson point process

annotations.note: `expMeasure r` is the exponential measure on ℝ with rate `r`, defined via `gammaMeasure 1 r`.

annotations.label: PDF of exponential distribution

anchor.snippet: The probability density function (pdf) of an exponential distribution is

annotations.note: `exponentialPDFReal r x = r * exp (-(r*x))` for `0 ≤ x` and `0` otherwise (via `exponentialPDF_eq`).

annotations.label: Rate parameter

anchor.snippet: Here λ > 0 is the parameter of the distribution, often called the rate parameter

annotations.note: The rate parameter is the `r : ℝ` argument of `exponentialPDFReal`/`expMeasure`, with positivity assumed via `hr : 0 < r`.

annotations.label: Infinite divisibility

anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution exhibits infinite divisibility

annotations.note: No notion of infinite divisibility or its instance for the exponential distribution was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: CDF of exponential distribution

anchor.snippet: The cumulative distribution function is given by

annotations.note: `cdf_expMeasure_eq` proves `cdf (expMeasure r) x = 1 - exp (-(r*x))` for `0 ≤ x` and `0` else.

annotations.label: Scale parameter parametrization

anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution is sometimes parametrized in terms of the scale parameter

annotations.note: Mathlib only provides the rate-parameter form; no scale-parameter alternative parametrization is defined.

anchors.snippet: The exponential distribution is sometimes parametrized in terms of the scale parameter

annotations.label: Mean of exponential distribution

anchor.snippet: The mean or expected value of an exponentially distributed random variable

annotations.note: No computation of `∫ x ∂(expMeasure r) = 1/r` is present in `Mathlib/Probability/Distributions/Exponential.lean`.

anchors.snippet: The mean or expected value of an exponentially distributed random variable

annotations.label: Memorylessness relation

anchor.section: Memorylessness property of exponential random variable
anchor.snippet: An exponentially distributed random variable T obeys the relation

annotations.note: No `memoryless` predicate or proof for the exponential distribution was found in Mathlib.

anchors.section: Memorylessness property of exponential random variable
anchors.snippet: An exponentially distributed random variable T obeys the relation

annotations.label: Characterization of memoryless distributions

anchor.section: Memorylessness property of exponential random variable
anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution and the geometric distribution are the only memoryless probability distributions

annotations.note: Memorylessness is not formalized, so no such characterization exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Constant failure rate characterization

anchor.section: Memorylessness property of exponential random variable
anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution is consequently also necessarily the only continuous probability distribution that has a constant failure rate

annotations.note: No notion of hazard/failure rate or its characterization is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantile function

anchor.snippet: The quantile function (inverse cumulative distribution function) for Exp

annotations.note: No quantile-function formula for the exponential distribution was found.

annotations.note: No interquartile range result for the exponential distribution was found.

annotations.label: Conditional Value at Risk

anchor.snippet: The conditional value at risk (CVaR) also known as the expected shortfall or superquantile for Exp

annotations.note: No CVaR/expected-shortfall definitions appear anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Buffered Probability of Exceedance

anchor.snippet: The buffered probability of exceedance is one minus the probability level at which the CVaR equals the threshold

annotations.note: No buffered probability of exceedance is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Minimum of independent exponentials

anchor.section: Distribution of the minimum of exponential random variables
anchor.snippet: Let X 1 , ..., X n be independent exponentially distributed random variables with rate parameters

annotations.note: No theorem that `min Xᵢ ∼ Exp(∑ λᵢ)` was found.

anchors.section: Distribution of the minimum of exponential random variables
anchors.snippet: Let X 1 , ..., X n be independent exponentially distributed random variables with rate parameters

annotations.label: Index of minimum is categorical

anchor.section: Distribution of the minimum of exponential random variables
anchor.snippet: The index of the variable which achieves the minimum is distributed according to the categorical distribution

annotations.note: Neither the categorical distribution nor this index theorem is formalized.

anchors.snippet: The index of the variable which achieves the minimum is distributed according to the categorical distribution

annotations.label: Difference not exponential

anchor.section: Distribution of the minimum of exponential random variables
anchor.snippet: is not exponentially distributed, if X 1 , ..., X n do not all have parameter 0

annotations.note: No negative result on the distribution of differences of exponentials was found.

annotations.label: Sum of two independent exponentials density

anchor.section: Sum of two independent exponential random variables
anchor.snippet: are independent exponential random variables with respective rate parameters

annotations.note: No convolution formula for two exponentials with different rates was found.

anchors.section: Sum of two independent exponential random variables
anchors.snippet: are independent exponential random variables with respective rate parameters

annotations.label: Equal rate parameters yield Erlang

anchor.section: Sum of two independent exponential random variables
anchor.snippet: In the case of equal rate parameters, the result is an Erlang distribution with shape 2

annotations.note: The Gamma distribution (which subsumes Erlang) is defined, but no theorem connects the sum of two exponentials to it.

annotations.label: Sum of n i.i.d. exponentials

anchor.section: Sum of two independent exponential random variables
anchor.snippet: The sum of n independent Exp

annotations.note: The Gamma target distribution exists in Mathlib, but the convolution identity sum of n iid Exp(λ) = Gamma(n,λ) is not proven.

annotations.label: Laplace absolute value

anchor.snippet: If X ~ Laplace

annotations.note: The Laplace distribution is not defined in Mathlib (no matching files found).

annotations.label: Uniform to exponential

anchor.snippet: If X ~ U (0, 1) then −log

annotations.note: No theorem stating that `-log U/λ ∼ Exp(λ)` for uniform `U` was found.

annotations.label: Log of Pareto

anchor.snippet: If X ~ Pareto

annotations.note: The Pareto distribution is formalized but no transformation result linking it to the exponential is present.

annotations.label: Limit of scaled beta

anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution is a limit of a scaled beta distribution

annotations.note: Beta distribution exists in Mathlib but no convergence-to-exponential limit theorem was found.

anchors.snippet: The exponential distribution is a limit of a scaled beta distribution

annotations.label: Special case of Pearson type 3

anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution is a special case of type 3 Pearson distribution

annotations.note: Pearson distributions are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Special case of Gamma

anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution is the special case of a Gamma distribution with shape parameter 1

annotations.note: By definition `exponentialPDFReal r x = gammaPDFReal 1 r x` and `expMeasure r = gammaMeasure 1 r`.

annotations.label: Closure under scaling

anchor.snippet: closure under scaling by a positive factor

annotations.note: No theorem stating that `kX ∼ Exp(λ/k)` for `X ∼ Exp(λ)` was found.

annotations.label: Benktander Weibull relation

anchor.snippet: 1 + X ~ BenktanderWeibull

annotations.note: The Benktander–Weibull distribution is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential of exponential is Pareto

anchor.snippet: ke X ~ Pareto

annotations.note: No transformation theorem relating `k·e^X` of an exponential to Pareto was found.

annotations.label: e to minus lambda X uniform
annotations.note: No such transformation theorem from exponential to uniform was found.

annotations.label: e to minus X is Beta

anchor.snippet: e −X ~ Beta

annotations.note: No `e^{-X} ∼ Beta` transformation result was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power law relation

anchor.snippet: e X ~ PowerLaw

annotations.note: Power-law distributions are not separately formalized.

anchor.snippet: ~ Gumbel

anchor.snippet: X has a chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom

annotations.label: Hoyt from exponential and arcsine

anchor.snippet: The Hoyt distribution can be obtained from exponential distribution and arcsine distribution

annotations.note: Neither Hoyt nor arcsine distributions appear in Mathlib's probability distributions.

annotations.label: Conjugate gamma prior

anchor.section: Bayesian inference with a conjugate prior
anchor.snippet: The conjugate prior for the exponential distribution is the gamma distribution

annotations.note: Bayesian conjugate-prior theory is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Posterior distribution

anchor.section: Bayesian inference with a conjugate prior
anchor.snippet: The posterior distribution p can then be expressed in terms of the likelihood function

annotations.note: No posterior-distribution derivation appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Posterior mean

anchor.section: Bayesian inference with a conjugate prior
anchor.snippet: The posterior mean here is

annotations.note: No posterior-mean computation for the exponential model exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Haar measure for exponential

anchor.section: Bayesian inference with a calibrating prior
anchor.snippet: the exponential has an associated Haar measure

annotations.note: Although Haar measure exists in Mathlib generally, the Bayesian Haar prior for the exponential distribution is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bayesian prediction with Haar prior

anchor.section: Bayesian inference with a calibrating prior
anchor.snippet: there is an exact expression for Bayesian predictions generated using the Haar prior

annotations.note: Bayesian predictive distributions are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inter-arrival times in Poisson process

anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution occurs naturally when describing the lengths of the inter-arrival times in a homogeneous Poisson process

annotations.note: Poisson point process theory and inter-arrival theorems are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous counterpart of geometric

anchor.snippet: The exponential distribution may be viewed as a continuous counterpart of the geometric distribution

annotations.note: Both distributions exist (`geometricPMFReal`, `expMeasure`) but no result connects them as continuous/discrete analogues.

annotations.label: Phone calls and radioactive decay

anchor.snippet: Similar caveats apply to the following examples which yield approximately exponentially distributed variables

annotations.note: Applied examples like phone calls and radioactive decay are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Constant probability per unit length

anchor.snippet: Exponential variables can also be used to model situations where certain events occur with a constant probability per unit length

annotations.note: Applied modelling examples are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Queuing theory service times

anchor.snippet: In queuing theory , the service times of agents in a system

annotations.note: Queueing-theory applications are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reliability theory bathtub curve

anchor.snippet: Reliability theory and reliability engineering also make extensive use of the exponential distribution

annotations.note: Reliability theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Barometric formula

anchor.snippet: the heights of the various molecules also follow an approximate exponential distribution, known as the Barometric formula

annotations.note: Barometric/physics examples are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hydrology extreme values

anchor.snippet: In hydrology , the exponential distribution is used to analyze extreme values

annotations.note: Hydrology/extreme-value applications are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Surgery duration

anchor.snippet: In operating-rooms management, the distribution of surgery duration

annotations.note: Operations-research applications are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Plug-in predictive distribution

anchor.snippet: the so-called plug-in distribution, formed by plugging a suitable estimate for the rate parameter

annotations.note: Plug-in predictive distributions are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the so-called plug-in distribution, formed by plugging a suitable estimate for the rate parameter

annotations.label: CNML predictive superiority

anchor.snippet: the CNML predictive distribution is strictly superior to the maximum likelihood plug-in distribution

annotations.note: CNML and predictive comparison theorems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inverse transform sampling

anchor.snippet: A conceptually very simple method for generating exponential variates is based on inverse transform sampling

annotations.note: Inverse transform sampling is not formalized for the exponential distribution in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Uniform symmetry trick

anchor.snippet: Moreover, if U is uniform on (0, 1), then so is 1 − U

annotations.note: No theorem stating `1 − U` is uniform on `(0,1)` for uniform `U` was found in `Mathlib/Probability/Distributions/Uniform.lean`.
